column1: # Polygon-based analysis — partner serviceable boundaries vs pair install
built via DBSCAN over hex cells above p30 install density
Test whether the partner's proven serviceable polygon (built from install history) is a stronger moderator of install success than raw `nearest_distance`.
(a) Of 2,561 pairs, how many are eligible (partner has a polygon)?
(b) Does being INSIDE vs OUTSIDE the polygon change install rate and reason mix?
(c) For pairs INSIDE, does depth (distance-from-edge / from-center) further differentiate?
Polygons vary in shape per partner — both raw meters and normalized (by equivalent_radius = sqrt(area_km2*1e6/pi)) are computed.
## 1b. How the polygon is actually built (supply-efficiency map)
se = installs / total     # total = installs + declines
color = crimson (se <= bad) | orange (se <= mid) | lightgreen (se > mid)
find_boundary.py keeps only 'lightgreen' + 'orange' hexes before DBSCAN.
CRIMSON hexes (partner tried, mostly declined) are EXCLUDED.
Implication: polygon is a SUPPLY-EFFICIENCY map, not raw install history.
- Two partners with same install count but different decline patterns
get DIFFERENT polygons — declines SHRINK the polygon.
- 'Inside' = partner has high SE here (CAN install + WILL try).
- Polygon is dynamic — updates with every install/decline decision.
- Pair booking coords from allocation_cohort.csv (same booking_location CTE as query_pcalls.txt).
- CRS: WGS84 for I/O, UTM zone 43N (EPSG:32643) for distance.
- Per pair, multiple partner polygons reduced via tie-break: deepest-containing > nearest-edge.
## 4. TABLE 1 — Inside vs Outside (of 2,499 pairs with polygon)
Install rate AND address_not_clear touch rate (pair ever touched ANC across calls, from reasons_union), with grand total row.
Outside pairs: LOWER install (38.6%) AND HIGHER ANC touch rate (48.2%). Both directions confirm polygon-outside = harder.
Within ANC-primary pairs specifically: inside 63.2% install vs outside 43.8% (+19.4pp gap).

Reason distribution is essentially stable inside vs outside (address_not_clear: 35.6% inside, 38.8% outside — 3pp shift). The polygon side does NOT change what partners talk about much — it changes whether the conversation converts to install.

Each eligible pair (n=2,499) lands in exactly one of 4 comm_quality × 2 address-family × 2 polygon_side = 16 cells. Address family = primary_first in {address_not_clear, address_too_far, address_wrong, building_access_issue, partner_reached_cant_find}.
Row totals (sanity): inside n = 1,939, outside n = 560, combined = 2,499 = 2,499 eligible pairs.
1. INSIDE BEATS OUTSIDE IN EVERY CELL — gap is +10 to +25pp across all 8 (comm × bucket) rows. The polygon-side effect is stable ACROSS comm quality and ACROSS address vs non-address.

2. Within each comm bucket, address-related pairs install BETTER than non-address-related ON BOTH SIDES of the polygon. Address friction is surmountable regardless of polygon side — it just resolves more often inside.

3. Bottom-of-the-barrel cell: not_applicable × non_address_related × outside = 58 pairs, 20.7% install. Non-address terminal friction (cancellations, unreachable, wrong_customer) combined with outside-polygon is the worst cocktail.

4. Top-of-the-barrel cells: clear × address_related × inside (68.1%, n=119) and not_applicable × address_related × inside (75.0%, small n=12). When the conversation was clean and the friction was address-family and the booking was inside the polygon — installs near-always happen.

5. mutual_failure × address_related shows the largest reliable gap (n=387 inside vs n=124 outside, +21pp). Even when both parties struggled on address, being inside the polygon recovered 60% of those pairs vs only 40% outside.
Totals drill-down — where the n's in the grid come from:
Polygon-eligible cohort: 2,499 pairs (= 2,561 parent cohort − 62 pairs whose partner has no polygon).
address_related total = 1,065 pairs, drilled down by primary_first:

Note: headline 927 (parent analysis) = strictly primary_first = address_not_clear. The address-family bucket used in the 5b grid is a broader superset of 5 primary_first reasons (1,089 at parent, 1,065 polygon-eligible after dropping 24 pairs without polygons — 20 of those are ANC).

non_address_related total = 1,434 pairs (= 2,499 − 1,065) — i.e. everything else (noise_or_empty, slot_confirmation, customer_postpone / cancelling / unreachable, wrong_customer, price_or_plan_query, duplicate, partner_postpone / no_show, install_site_technical, payment, competitor, router, other).
## 6. address_not_clear pairs: install rate by side
Inside polygon, address friction is surmountable 63% of the time.
Address friction APPEARS at similar rate inside vs outside (35.6% vs 38.8%), but RESOLVES much better inside.
Each quintile row: pairs, metric range, install_rate_%, anc_rate_% (ANC touch from reasons_union). Grand total at end of each metric.
-- (2a) by dist_edge_m — raw meters; Q1=shallowest, Q5=deepest --
-- (2c) by dist_center_m — raw meters; Q1=near center, Q5=far --
-- (2d) by norm_dist_center — inverse signal; Q1=near center, Q5=far --
Q1 -> Q5 gaps across the four metrics:
Edge-distance and center-distance are INVERSE signals (deep inside ≈ close to center). Both point the same direction: depth inside proven territory = higher install + less address friction.
Normalized > raw (Q1->Q5 gaps 9-11pp vs 5-10pp). Varying polygon shapes need size-normalization.
ANC touch rate inside vs outside (44% vs 48%) is a small 4pp gap. Install-rate gap (16.7pp) is much bigger. Polygon's role is primarily whether friction RESOLVES, not whether it APPEARS.

Second-pass classifier (flag_address_chain.py) tags each call against the Delhi address-resolution hierarchy LANDMARK -> GALI -> FLOOR. addr_chain_stuck_at_mode = dominant stuck-point across the pair's calls (na if the pair wasn't address-talking, else which step broke).
(a) Distribution of addr_chain_stuck_at_mode is stable inside vs outside. Chain engagement happens at SIMILAR rates regardless of polygon side (col % of 2,499 eligible pairs):
Confirms polygon side doesn't change WHAT partners discuss (stable reason mix AND stable chain engagement).

HEADLINE: gali-stuck × outside = 25.4% install. Gali-stuck × inside = 62.5%. +37.1pp gap — the biggest single-cell gap anywhere in the analysis. Lane-level ambiguity combined with no partner-polygon-coverage is the worst possible combination.

Other takeaways: every stuck_at level shows a larger install gap by polygon side than the headline +16.7pp (which is the unconditioned average). The polygon effect compounds with chain-engagement difficulty.

Chain engagement adds +11.2pp install INSIDE (62.4% vs 51.2%) but only +2.7pp OUTSIDE (40.3% vs 37.6%). The 'chain engagement is protective' finding from the parent analysis is ALMOST ENTIRELY an INSIDE-POLYGON phenomenon. Outside the polygon, engaging the chain barely helps — you can agree on every landmark/gali/floor and still not install.
(d) Within primary_first=address_not_clear (907 eligible ANC pairs), install rate by stuck_at × side:
Gali-stuck × outside × ANC = 47 pairs, 21.3% install — sharpest 'bottom of the barrel' signature. +41.2pp gap within ANC for gali-stuck.
2. Inside-polygon pairs install at 55.3% vs outside at 38.6% — +16.7pp.
3. Within address_not_clear pairs, gap widens to +19.4pp (63% vs 44%).
4. Depth inside polygon differentiates further: shallow 44% -> deep 57% on normalized edge-distance.
5. Reason distribution barely shifts inside vs outside (~3pp on ANC). Polygon side does not change WHAT partners talk about; it changes whether the conversation RESOLVES to install.

Promise Maker: polygon-containment is a stronger serviceability signal than geometric nearest_distance. If a booking lands outside every partner's polygon, the promise is already on weaker ground. Consider soft-gating or penalty in Promise Maker upstream of Allocation.
Allocation: prefer partners whose polygon contains the booking (>=55% install baseline) over those whose polygon doesn't (<=39% baseline). Rank further by depth-inside, not just containment.
## 9b. Geoff + Donna critique — circularity softened, not dissolved
Both agents were briefed twice: once on the original framing, once
after the SE correction. Convergent conclusion:
- Before SE correction: 'install history predicts install history' —
- After SE correction: 'Shifted from installs predict installs to
install-RATE predicts install-RATE. Less circular, not acircular.
The denominator (attempts) does the real disentanglement work.'
- Decisive test: Mar-only holdout. If 16.7pp gap collapses -> memorization.
If gap >= 10pp persists -> SE captures something temporally stable.
- Additional: stratify Mar by whether partner had ANY Jan/Feb attempts
in that hex. Pairs encountering a NEW hex in Mar = cleanest test.
- Depth gradient (+9.3pp) is coherence signal with SE weighting, but
still partly density-driven. Treat as suggestive.
- Absorbing-crimson trap: SE decay is event-driven, not time-driven.
Once crimson, Allocation stops sending leads -> no attempts ->
- Need gentle TIME-DECAY on hex confidence — hexes with no attempts
in N days should regress toward prior.
- Compound-signal problem: polygon conflates CAN (tacit knowledge)
+ WILL (motivation/viability). Gating destroys ability to diagnose
which is failing. Need decline-reason logging at hex level.
- ANC 4.3pp delta: real, small — true delta probably 3-6pp same
direction. Not noise; weak confirmation pending more pairs.
- Cleanest finding (per Donna): reason mix is STABLE inside vs outside
(3pp shift). The polygon doesn't change WHAT partners talk about —
it changes whether the conversation RESOLVES to install. Tacit-
1. DO NOT use polygon as hard gate in Promise Maker.
2. DO use as SOFT FEATURE in Allocation, DECOMPOSED:
3. Reserve ~10% of lead volume for sub-threshold hexes adjacent to
green — the exploration quota — to break the crimson ratchet.
4. Balancing metric: decline rate in exploration-quota hexes over
time. Falls -> CAN was issue, intervention works. Flat -> WILL
was issue, gating wasn't the lever.
T3: Install rate of 62 no-polygon pairs
column2: Cold-start canary — if ~38% polygon does work, if ~55% it's experience correlate

column1: T4: Regress ANC on hex install-density
column2: Does depth gradient survive density controls? (Donna)

column1: Polygons are a Feb 2026 snapshot; cohort is Jan-Mar 2026. Mar pairs don't have their own installs reflected in the polygon.
62 pairs (2.4%) have partners without polygons — newer / low-volume partners below the p30 hex-density threshold.
2 pairs lack booking coords (serviceability CTE didn't resolve).
A partner's polygon is a union of DBSCAN clusters; we pick one per pair via deepest-containing > nearest-edge tie-break.
